coverage_criteria: Provides their current role or title, such as job title, academic position, or equivalent.
States how long they have been in their current role or position (in years, months, etc.).
Names a field, industry, or area (e.g., AI, healthcare, finance). A broad field name is sufficient — do NOT probe for sub-specialties, research focus, or sub-areas within the field.

subtopics.description: Typical Week Description: the participant's initial description of what their typical week looks like, establishing the base task list.
coverage_criteria: STEP 1 (week recall) — REQUIRED: The participant has described what their typical week of work looks like, including the kinds of tasks they actually do. This is satisfied if they described their day-to-day or weekly work pattern in ANY prior response — do not re-ask if it was already answered during role/context. Ask a week-reconstruction question (e.g., 'Can you walk me through what a typical week looks like?', 'What did you work on yesterday?') only if no such description exists yet. The question MUST be anchored in time and actions — do NOT ask about responsibilities, ownership, or role identity (e.g., do NOT ask 'what are your core responsibilities', 'what's really yours vs the lab's', 'how do you see your role'). Those belong to Role and Context, not Task Inventory.

Every task in the inventory should have at least two components: an ACTION (what the participant does) and an OBJECT (what they act on). An OBJECTIVE (why — the goal or outcome) is helpful but optional. The interviewer MUST attempt to infer missing details from prior context before asking. If action+object are clear, treat the task as covered even when purpose is broad, variable, or hard to articulate (e.g., 'it depends', 'to answer their question'). Ask about objective only when it is genuinely needed to disambiguate the task, and ask at most once.

subtopics.description: Task List Completion: sweeping for lower-cadence tasks, confirming the list is exhaustive, and ensuring each task has a clear action and object (objective optional).
coverage_criteria: STEP 2 (frequency sweep) — REQUIRED: Ask exactly one cadence-specific question about lower-frequency work (monthly/quarterly/few-times-a-year). The question must reference cadence/frequency directly. Do NOT use a generic catch-all like 'anything else?' as the primary wording.

STEP 3 (closure rule) — CONDITIONAL after STEP 2: If the participant gives a clear no-new-work response (e.g., 'no', 'that's it', 'nothing else', 'just that'), mark this subtopic complete immediately. Do NOT ask another catch-all, completeness, or rephrased variant. Also treat explicit repetition/frustration signals with no new tasks (e.g., 'you keep asking this', 'am I not giving enough detail?') as closure and move on.

COMPLETION — this subtopic is DONE when the most recent cadence sweep/closure response adds no new tasks. If the participant introduces new work, capture clear action/object (and objective when available) for the new task(s), then ask one final completion confirmation only if needed.
If the user gives two consecutive sub-5-word replies with no new task information, stop probing and treat the completeness confirmation as passed.

Every task in the inventory should have at least two components: an ACTION (what the participant does) and an OBJECT (what they act on). An OBJECTIVE (why — the goal or outcome) is helpful but optional. The interviewer MUST attempt to infer missing details from prior context before asking. If action+object are clear, treat the task as covered even when purpose is broad, variable, or hard to articulate (e.g., 'it depends', 'to answer their question'). Ask about objective only when it is genuinely needed to disambiguate the task, and ask at most once.

subtopics.description: Priority tasks: the task(s) the participant considers most important in their work.
coverage_criteria: STEP 1 (open ask) — REQUIRED: The Interviewer has asked the participant, open-endedly, which task feels most important to them (e.g., 'Which of those feels most important to you?' or 'Which one matters most?'). Ask this once regardless of how many priorities the participant names.

STEP 2 (direct confirmation) — REQUIRED only when needed: Confirm main priority once with direct wording (e.g., 'Would you say that's your main priority right now?'). Do NOT use 'anything else on the same level' phrasing. If the participant already gave a clear priority answer (including high-stakes framing such as 'presenting and writing are high stakes' or equivalent), treat STEP 2 as satisfied without asking another question.

COMPLETION: Once STEP 1 is answered and STEP 2 is either explicitly asked once or implicitly satisfied by a clear singular priority answer, this subtopic is DONE. Move on immediately and do NOT re-open priority ranking.
For each priority task, capture a brief high-level description of what it involves. Do NOT require a detailed step-by-step workflow during intake.

subtopics.description: Time allocation: how much time they spend on each task, as a percentage of their total work week. A time-split widget is shown to the participant to collect the actual percentages. IMPORTANT: The interviewer MUST send a message targeting this subtopic — for example, 'Now let's see how your time breaks down across these tasks — here's a quick widget for you to fill in.' This message triggers the widget. Do NOT ask for per-task percentages one by one in conversation. Do NOT skip this subtopic even if task-list completeness was already confirmed in the Breadth subtopic.
coverage_criteria: PREREQUISITE: The task list has been confirmed complete per the COMPLETION criterion of the Task List Completion subtopic — i.e. the most recent completeness confirmation was answered without introducing any new tasks. Do NOT proceed to this subtopic if the participant's last reply to a completeness question introduced new work. If the task list is not yet confirmed complete, ask the completeness question first.

The participant has provided explicit percentage or fraction breakdowns showing how their work time is divided across tasks — for example, 'I spend about 50% on X and 30% on Y', or a submitted widget with per-task percentages. Evaluate this criterion by scanning the conversation for explicit numeric time splits: if NO percentages or fractions appear in the participant's messages, this criterion is FALSE regardless of any other confirmations. A 'yes' or 'no that's it' reply to a completeness question does NOT satisfy this criterion.

Grouped categories are valid for time allocation (the split does NOT need to map 1:1 to every Task Inventory line item). If the provided percentages plausibly cover a full typical week (approximately 95-105% total, or explicitly stated as complete), mark this subtopic complete and move on immediately.

Do NOT ask post-split follow-up questions in this subtopic once a plausible breakdown is provided. Specifically avoid representativeness/completeness prompts like 'does that breakdown feel representative of most weeks?' or 'what isn't captured there?'. Record the split and move on immediately. Only if a required number is missing or inconsistent may you ask one targeted clarification about that specific gap.
